headline_assessment: Daejeon Zero O'Clock Festival should be treated as a mixed-verdict case: official visitor/economic/3-no-risk signals are strong, but traffic disruption, budget transparency, local identity and economic-effect methodology remain contested.
confidence: B+ for official success metrics and price-prevention governance; C/B for opposition and civic-inconvenience critiques depending on source type.

short_answer.why_success_candidate: Daejeon City reported more than two million visitors in 2024, a 3-no festival record around safety incidents, trash and price gouging, high SNS reach and estimated total economic effect of 403.3 billion KRW.
short_answer.why_failure_or_risk_candidate: Critics question whether headline visitor/economic-effect metrics reflect local merchants' lived outcome, whether large budget input was transparent and efficient, and whether long central-road closure created disproportionate civic inconvenience.
short_answer.what_to_collect_next: Official evaluation report, raw visitor-count method, contract/budget execution, card-sales analysis, merchant survey, traffic-control complaints and public transport capacity data.

evidence.claim: Daejeon City said the 2024 festival drew more than two million visitors, almost double the previous year's 1.1 million, using thermal-sensor unmanned counters.
Daejeon City framed the festival as a two-year 3-no festival around safety incidents, trash and price gouging, and estimated 403.3 billion KRW total economic effect.
The mayor apologized for daily-life inconvenience caused by full vehicle control on Jungang-ro and Daejong-ro sections and said shortcomings would be improved.

A media analysis framed the festival's success or failure as dependent on evaluation criteria: the city emphasizes visitors and economic effect, while critics emphasize local identity, budget transparency and civic inconvenience.
The same analysis cites a political forum claim that only 23 percent of surveyed small merchants said sales rose, while 71 percent said sales fell.
A public-opinion article argued that subway extension, circular buses and parking measures did not fully resolve structural transport limits, including a single subway line and 10-minute intervals.
For 2025, Daejeon signed an agreement with eight downtown merchant associations for price gouging prevention, kindness and fair-trade culture.

why_chain.label: Official success metrics
why_chain.finding: Visitors, economic effect, SNS reach and 3-no festival framing create a strong success narrative.

why_chain.label: Measurement challenge
why_chain.finding: Visitor counting and economic-effect estimation must be audited against methodology, duplication, direct/indirect effects and merchant-level outcomes.

why_chain.label: Civic inconvenience
why_chain.finding: Long central-road closures and transit constraints can reduce resident satisfaction even if tourist attendance rises.

why_chain.label: Price and merchant governance
why_chain.finding: Merchant association agreements and price-display commitments are preventive controls, not proof of economic benefit.

why_chain.label: Mixed verdict
why_chain.finding: The case should not be labeled success or failure without preserving both official outcome signals and counter-evidence.

documents_to_collect_next: Final 2024 evaluation report and raw methodology for visitor counting
Budget execution, contract packages and outsourced operating scopes
Card-sales and merchant-survey data by zone and industry
Traffic-control plan, bus rerouting, subway extension capacity and complaint logs
2025 price agreement implementation results and inspection records
